specific_mechanism: Bauschinger effect: in a metal that has been plastically deformed in one direction, the yield strength upon reverse loading is asymmetrically reduced (typically 20-50%) due to back-stress accumulation from dislocation pile-ups against grain boundaries; modeled in plasticity theory as kinematic hardening (shifting yield surface center) rather than isotropic hardening (uniform expansion).
llm_application: Treat RLHF in one direction (e.g., a safety push reducing harmful outputs on category A) as 'forward loading' that accumulates 'back stress' in the model's prompt-embedding-shifted-centroid space. Predict the asymmetric reduction in 'reverse-direction yield' (i.e., over-yielding / over-correction on adjacent prompts in the reverse direction) using a kinematic-hardening-style model where the back-stress is a measurable activation summary rather than a free-floating empirical observation. Cyclic RLHF (safety push → helpfulness push → safety push) would then be predictably modeled as cyclic plasticity with workhardening stagnation and fatigue limits.
novelty_claim: Empirical asymmetric softening from RLHF is documented (arxiv 2504.09757, 2502.17424, 2310.03693, 2502.11555). Asymmetric confidence penalties are deployed (2602.21420). Gradient-surgery approaches address conflict (2508.07172). But none formalize the phenomenon via a Bauschinger / kinematic-hardening frame with back-stress as a measurable state and predictive scaling laws. The contribution would be a quantitative cyclic-plasticity model of RLHF, not just a label.
